title: Linux systemd Service Management
description: Learn to manage system services with systemctl and inspect logs with journalctl on modern Linux distributions.

tasks.hint: Use systemctl start: 'sudo systemctl start nginx'
tasks.explanation: 'systemctl start' activates a unit (service) right away without changing its enabled/disabled state at boot.

tasks.hint: Use systemctl stop: 'sudo systemctl stop apache2'
tasks.explanation: 'systemctl stop' gracefully shuts down a running service unit.

tasks.hint: Use systemctl restart: 'sudo systemctl restart mysql'
tasks.explanation: 'systemctl restart' is commonly used after configuration changes to apply them by reloading the service.

tasks.prompt: Enable the 'docker' service to start automatically at boot.
tasks.hint: Use systemctl enable: 'sudo systemctl enable docker'
tasks.explanation: 'systemctl enable' creates the necessary symlinks so the service starts on system boot.

tasks.prompt: Check the detailed status of the 'ssh' service.
tasks.explanation: 'systemctl status' shows whether the unit is active, recent log lines, and its load state.

tasks.prompt: Reload systemd manager configuration after editing unit files.
tasks.explanation: 'daemon-reload' must be run after creating or modifying unit files so systemd picks up the changes.

tasks.prompt: Follow systemd journal logs in real time (like tail -f).
tasks.explanation: 'journalctl -f' streams new journal entries as they are written, useful for live troubleshooting.

tasks.prompt: View all journal logs for the 'nginx' service unit since today.
tasks.hint: Use journalctl with unit and since: 'journalctl -u nginx --since today'
tasks.explanation: 'journalctl -u' filters by unit name. '--since today' limits output to entries from the current day.
